questions.question: Define deadlock detection in operating systems. Why is it necessary to detect deadlocks rather than prevent them?
questions.hint: Think about the difference between prevention/avoidance and detection approaches
keyPoints: Deadlock detection is the process of identifying whether a set of processes are in a deadlocked state
It is necessary because deadlocks cannot always be prevented through system design
Detection allows the system to recover and continue operation
Necessary when resources can be dynamically allocated and deadlock conditions may arise
Enables system to maintain efficiency by not imposing overly restrictive conditions

questions.question: What is a Wait-for Graph? Explain how it is used for deadlock detection in systems with single instance resources.
questions.hint: Remember: cycle in wait-for graph indicates deadlock for single instance resources
keyPoints: Wait-for Graph is a directed graph where nodes represent processes
An edge Pi → Pj exists if process Pi is waiting for a resource held by process Pj
A deadlock exists if and only if the wait-for graph contains a cycle
It is applicable only when each resource type has a single instance
The operating system periodically checks for cycles in this graph

questions.question: State the conditions required for invoking the deadlock detection algorithm in an operating system.
questions.hint: Recall the data structures needed for the detection algorithm
keyPoints: System must have information about current allocation of resources
Request matrix showing current resource requests by each process
Allocation matrix showing currently allocated resources
Number of processes and resource types in the system
Available vector showing currently available resources

questions.question: Differentiate between deadlock detection and deadlock recovery.
questions.hint: Think of detection as 'diagnosis' and recovery as 'treatment'
keyPoints: Detection identifies if deadlock exists in the current state
Recovery takes action after deadlock has been detected
Detection is passive - it only identifies the problem
Recovery is active - it resolves the deadlock situation
Both are used when prevention/avoidance strategies are not implemented

questions.question: What is the 'Ostrich Algorithm' for handling deadlocks? Why might it be used in practice?
questions.hint: This is a humorous approach named after bird behavior
keyPoints: It is an approach to ignore deadlock problems altogether
Name comes from the ostrich putting its head in the sand
Used when deadlocks are rare and cost of prevention is too high
Practical in systems where occasional crashes are acceptable
Database systems and some operating systems use this approach

questions.question: Explain resource preemption as a method of deadlock recovery. What are its drawbacks?
questions.hint: Think about what happens when you forcibly take resources from a process
keyPoints: Resources are forcibly taken from some processes and given to others
The operating system selects a victim process and preempts its resources
Selected process may need to be rolled back to a safe state
Drawback: May cause data corruption if resources are taken unexpectedly
Drawback: Starvation is possible if same process is repeatedly selected

questions.question: Explain the deadlock detection algorithm for systems with multiple instances of each resource type. Trace through the algorithm with a suitable example.
questions.hint: Use the Banker's algorithm style data structures and trace step by step
keyPoints: Data structures: Available vector, Allocation matrix, Request matrix
Algorithm steps: 1) Initialize Work = Available, 2) Find process where Request ≤ Work
3) Mark process as finished and release resources (Work = Work + Allocation)
4) Repeat until all processes are found or no such process exists
If any process is not finished after algorithm, system is in deadlock
The unfinished processes constitute the deadlock set
Algorithm complexity is O(m × n²) where m = resource types, n = processes

questions.question: Compare and contrast the three methods of recovering from deadlock: (i) Process Termination, (ii) Resource Preemption, and (iii) System Restart.
questions.hint: Consider the pros and cons of each approach from system perspective
keyPoints: Process Termination: Abort one or more processes to break deadlock
Two approaches: Abort all deadlocked processes or abort one at a time
Criteria for selection: Priority, execution time, resource usage, restartability
Resource Preemption: Take resources away from processes and give to others
Requires rollback mechanism to return process to safe state
System Restart: Complete system shutdown and reboot
Most drastic method, used as last resort
Trade-offs: Data loss, starvation, system downtime considerations

questions.question: Discuss the criteria for selecting a victim process during deadlock recovery. Explain the concept of rollback and checkpointing.
questions.hint: Think about fairness and cost considerations in victim selection
keyPoints: Selection criteria: Process priority, execution time remaining, resources held
Number of deadlocks caused by the process historically
Cost of terminating vs cost of continuing
Rollback: Returning process to a previously saved safe state
Checkpointing: Periodically saving process state to stable storage
After rollback, process must restart from checkpointed state
Prevents data loss and allows process to continue from safe state

questions.question: Design a deadlock detection algorithm for a system with multiple resource types. Include the data structures required and explain each step of the algorithm.
questions.hint: This is similar to Banker's algorithm but without safety check
keyPoints: Data structures: Available[m], Allocation[n][m], Request[n][m]
Available: Vector of available resources of each type
Allocation: Matrix showing resources allocated to each process
Request: Matrix showing current request of each process
Algorithm: Initialize work = available, find process meeting condition
Process Pi is eligible if Request[i] ≤ Work
Mark eligible process as finished, release its resources
Repeat until no more processes can finish
Deadlock exists if not all processes can finish

questions.question: Consider a system with 3 processes P0, P1, P2 and two resource types A (3 instances) and B (2 instances). Given: Allocation matrix = [[1,0], [1,1], [0,1]] and Request matrix = [[1,0], [0,1], [1,0]]. Available = [1,0]. Determine using the deadlock detection algorithm whether the system is in a deadlocked state.
questions.hint: Trace through the detection algorithm step by step
keyPoints: Step 1: Work = [1,0], Initialize Finish = [false, false, false]
Step 2: Check P0: Request[0]=[1,0] ≤ Work=[1,0], so P0 can finish
Step 3: Work = [1,0] + [1,0] = [2,0], Finish[0]=true
Step 4: Check P1: Request[1]=[0,1] ≤ Work=[2,0]? NO
Step 5: Check P2: Request[2]=[1,0] ≤ Work=[2,0], so P2 can finish
Step 6: Work = [2,0] + [0,1] = [2,1], Finish[2]=true
Step 7: Check P1 again: Request[1]=[0,1] ≤ Work=[2,1], so P1 can finish
Step 8: All processes can finish, so NO DEADLOCK

questions.question: Given the following resource allocation state: 3 processes P1, P2, P3; Resource type R with 4 instances. Allocation: P1=1, P2=1, P3=1. Request: P1=2, P2=1, P3=2. Available: 1. Using the detection algorithm, determine if there is a deadlock and identify the processes involved.
keyPoints: Step 1: Work = 1, Finish = [false, false, false]
Step 2: P1: Request=2 > Work=1, cannot finish
Step 3: P2: Request=1 ≤ Work=1, P2 can finish
Step 4: Work = 1 + 1 = 2, Finish[2] = true
Step 5: P1: Request=2 ≤ Work=2, P1 can finish
Step 6: Work = 2 + 1 = 3, Finish[1] = true
Step 7: P3: Request=2 ≤ Work=3, P3 can finish
Step 8: All processes finish, NO DEADLOCK
System is in safe state

questions.question: A system has 4 processes P0, P1, P2, P3 and 2 resource types A(2 instances), B(1 instance). Current state: Allocation: [[1,0], [0,1], [1,1], [0,0]]; Request: [[1,0], [1,0], [0,0], [1,1]]; Available: [0,0]. Determine if deadlock exists and explain with wait-for graph analysis.
questions.hint: Check if any process can finish with Available=[0,0]
keyPoints: Step 1: Work = [0,0], Finish = [false, false, false, false]
Step 2: No process has Request ≤ Work (all have non-zero requests)
Step 3: Since no process can proceed, algorithm terminates
Finish = [false, false, false, false]
Deadlock exists involving all 4 processes
Wait-for graph: P0→P1 (P0 waiting for P1's A), P1→P2 (waiting for A), P2→P0 (waiting for B), P3→P0 (waiting for A)
Cycle exists in wait-for graph: P0→P1→P2→P0
Conclusion: System is in DEADLOCK

questions.question: How frequently should deadlock detection be performed? What factors influence this decision?
questions.hint: Think about cost-benefit analysis of detection frequency
keyPoints: Frequency depends on how often deadlocks may occur
If deadlocks are frequent, detect more often to minimize affected processes
Detection has overhead - more frequent = more overhead
Consider number of processes in system
Consider resources available - more resources = less frequent detection needed
Trade-off between responsiveness and system performance

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'communication deadlock' and how it differs from resource deadlock. How can detection be performed for communication deadlocks?
questions.hint: Think about message passing systems vs resource allocation systems
keyPoints: Communication deadlock occurs in distributed systems
Processes wait for messages from each other (communication channel)
Differs from resource deadlock which involves physical resources
Also called 'message passing' deadlock
Conditions: Mutual waiting for message exchange
Detection: Can use wait-for graph with message channels
Timeout-based detection: Process waits for message beyond threshold
Control message passing to detect cyclic waiting
More complex in distributed systems due to lack of global state
